summary: Paul Graham explains that his heroes are not the most admirable people in history but the individuals and even a machine that personally influenced him. He finds a clear pattern: each hero cares intensely about their work and is uncompromisingly honest, refusing to pander. The essay uses portraits of these figures to illustrate how devotion, integrity, and originality shape lasting impact.
key_points: His heroes are defined by personal influence, not universal greatness.
The common thread is obsessive care for the work and a refusal to pander.
True creativity often serves exploration rather than audience approval.
Greatness comes from pursuing big, unfamiliar questions with rigor.
Taste and integrity can be decisive forces in design and leadership.
money_quotes: After I made the list, I looked to see if there was a pattern, and there was, a very clear one.
Everyone on the list had two qualities: they cared almost excessively about their work, and they were absolutely honest.
Much of the best stuff isn't made for audiences, but for oneself.
To do really great things, you have to seek out questions people didn't even realize were questions.
You only get one life.  Why not do something huge?
key_insight: Lasting influence comes from obsessive devotion to the work and a refusal to pander, even when the audience is not in view.
